data.title: Helen of Troy: Ignore The Seemingly Good Value
data.description:
Ugly Portrait

We agree that Helen of Troy Ltd. (HELE) deserves the strong sell Quant Rating assigned to the stock by Seeking Alpha. We do not foresee any catalyst for a turnaround. The shortfall in revenue and earnings, the worrisome cash flow from operations, and the reduced cash on hand since the end of FY ’22 are likely to exacerbate, in our opinion. Internal problems for the company, trade, and supply issues are going to linger and hurt any turnaround effort. Management is offering no guidance for FY '26, dampening investors’ interests and essentially stifling any uptick in momentum. There is so little interest and trading in Helen of Troy that the Levered/Unlevered Beta is at 0.19.

Complicating hopes for a turnaround after the shares dived -85% over the previous 5 years, down 73.4% over the last 12 months, and -53.35% YTD, is the turmoil in the C-suite marked by the abrupt departure of Helen’s CEO “effective immediately,” per the May 2, ’25 announcement. Adding to the negativity is Seeking Alpha’s recent red notice warning highlighting Helen’s declining growth and “The company has CAPEX Growth (YoY) of -50.1% while the Consumer Discretionary sector median is -3.33%.” 13 out of 13 metrics for growth graded by Seeking Alpha for growth get near failing grade.

The kerfuffle between nations is causing uncertain times for Helen of Troy and the stock. The former CEO told shareholders America’s global trade policy is causing “significant uncertainty and disruption to our business.” Helen needs to “diversify its supply chain and reduce costs,” move its home and outdoor, beauty, and wellness consumer products manufacturing out of China to “mitigate 70%-80% of the tariff impacts…”

Helen of Troy lost its mojo as it worked its way out of the pandemic years. The stock was approximately $240 per share at the close of 2021, then dropped 100 points over the year to close 2023 at about $140 per share. Revenue reportedly fell $2.22B to $2B, and for 2025 is expected to be around $1.9B. Gross profit was $953.2M in 2022 but will just top $914M in 2025. Earnings from continuing operations will be less than half in 2025 ($123.8M) of what they were in 2022 ($253.9M). Cash and receivables are also less than in previous years, while long-term debt is up from $659.4M at the end of 2024 to ~$917.5M at the last report. Even Helen’s most recent acquisition, Olive and June, had declining sales as reported in Q4 ’25. Despite the recent share price rise of ~18% during the first week of June ’25, the short interest ratio tops 11%; days to cover dropped from about 5 in March to 2.8 on May 15. By comparison, shorts had 15.8 days to cover on June 14, ’24.

Valuation

On September ’24, we rated the stock a speculative buy at about $59 per share. It subsequently climbed to +$74 each. 9 months later, we figure a fair price to be about $28 per share by multiplying the PE of 5.13 times a forecast $5.44 EPS for FY ’25. Using the FY ’24 EPS figure, a fair price for the stock would be $36.7. Ours is on the high end of speculative EPS among analysts. Analysts using various Discounted Cash Flow models and exits peg Helen of Troy’s intrinsic value from $43 to $119 per share.

Risks

The intrinsic valuation might help determine a fair price for a sugar daddy interested in acquiring the brand names, Helen’s greatest assets, plus its retail outlets and the success of management’s investments in online sales. In our pessimistic opinion, the shares will move between the mid-to-high $20s for a long time to come, while the PE hovers at a low 5.13, the supply chain is threatened, and future sales and earnings forecasts are on hold. Other problems the company faces include finding brand names that ensure a growth platform.

Olive and June's sales were less than expected per the last earnings report. The brand marketing of beauty products is changing with outreach to Gen Z. They are susceptible to influencers, product safety, and, as one observer put it to Fairchild Media, “getting a good deal.” Consumer spending is still rising, but so are retail prices, as producers are passing along the cost of tariffs. These conditions have the potential to hurt margins or increase inflation, which, in either scenario, does not bode well for Helen of Troy over the next few quarters or longer.

Revlon emerged from bankruptcy in 2023; can lenders who took over and are reorganizing the beauty business build the brand the way marketing and make-up specialists did? Please note that Olive and June’s subscription membership, trending nail online shopping logo, is not among the brands promoted on Helen’s website. The Helen management must be able to mitigate consumer loss risks, first, to dividend-paying stocks and in the field to cheaper knock-offs. Finally, Helen pays no dividend, and in uncertain times, that is what retail value investors consider when investing. The next earnings report is scheduled to be announced on July 8, 2025.

There is some risk in adopting a strong sell judgment when the stock is low-priced. Insiders made 37 open-market buys this past year, with few sales reported. Hedge funds made small trades in the last three months, but fewer funds own Helen of Troy stock in 2025 than in the previous 5 quarters.

Debt presents another risk to be considered as a worrisome risk. The debt-to-equity ratio is 54.5% and has increased from 30% over 5 years. Operating cash flow does not cover the debt payments, but EBIT does cover the debt.

Takeaway

Helen of Troy is a profitable company, and its stock price is enticing, but it deserves a strong sell rating for retail value investors. Buying shares at this good valuation is risky because of the intrinsic and extrinsic turmoil. Neither insiders nor funds are flocking to the stock.

Margins are higher than for the Consumer Discretion sector median, except for cash per share and the asset turnover ratio. Growth potential and momentum are keeping a lid on the share price, and since the firm pays no dividend, this is not a worthwhile investment for retail value investors. The share price descent cannot be turned around quickly and without pain. In December ’21, the company’s market cap touched $6B, while it currently figures to be $633M. We believe the company is liquid enough to cover liabilities when they are due, but, in our opinion, the investment it will take in marketing the brand names to new generations of consumers is going to be a hefty undertaking.

data.title: GoodRx: Stabilizing Revenue Amid Healthy Profit Gains
data.description:
Though the stock market has continued to glide upward and recoup the losses since Trump’s tariffs were announced, I continue to believe that dispersion will increase - particularly, that the large-cap stocks that have dominated the market over the last few years will see a correction in their valuations, while value stocks in the small cap space shine after years of underperformance.

GoodRx (NASDAQ:GDRX), a prescription drug savings company, is a great and little-known small cap value stock. Under new leadership, the company is adapting its go-to market playbook and stabilizing its revenue, while continuing to grow adjusted EBITDA. Down ~10% since the start of January and ~50% over the trailing twelve months, investors certainly need patience here - but this is a great stock to bank on for a rebound.

I last wrote a buy opinion on GoodRx in March, upgrading it from a prior neutral rating when it was trading in the mid-$4s. Since then, the company has slightly accelerated its pace of revenue growth, buoyed by its strategy of forging direct relationships with pharmaceutical companies. It has also increased its full-year adjusted EBITDA outlook. Amid its declining valuation, I’m reiterating my buy rating on this stock.

In my view, these are the core reasons to be bullish on GoodRx:

  • GoodRx’s revenue base can almost be considered recurring. Since the company primarily deals in subscription drugs, many consumers who utilize GoodRx promotions will keep coming back to refill their prescriptions, earning the company a recurring slice of that transaction.
  • Broadening distribution channels. The company’s sales from its pivot to direct pharmaceutical relationships are growing in the double digits. This channel gives GoodRx access to a new set of consumers and at favorable margins.
  • Compelling adjusted EBITDA margins. Even amid weaker revenue growth, GoodRx is managing to maintain mid-30s adjusted EBITDA margins and generating consistent profitability gains.
  • Potential buyout candidate. Though I never base an investment decision solely on a company’s potential to be acquired, GoodRx has all the right traits of a company fit to be acquired: a small and undemanding market cap, rich adjusted EBITDA, and a niche business with a recurring clientele.

Perhaps the biggest advantage to investing in this stock, of course, is its cheap price: at current share prices near $4, GoodRx trades at a market cap of $1.51 billion. After we net off the $301.0 million of cash and $485.8 million of debt on GoodRx’s latest balance sheet, the company’s resulting enterprise value is $1.69 billion.

For FY25, GoodRx has maintained its full-year revenue outlook at $810-$840 million, or 2-6% growth. However, it has slightly nudged up its adjusted EBITDA guidance to $273-$287 million, the midpoint of which implies a 33.9% margin.

This puts GoodRx’s valuation multiples at:

  • 2.0x EV/FY25 revenue
  • 6.0x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA

GoodRx’s 6x adjusted EBITDA multiple is more appropriate to a company that is at risk of seeing revenue declines or has a major headwind to margins - neither of which apply here. While acknowledging that the company is going through a challenging business transition, investors still have an opportunity to buy into this stock for a healthy rebound.

Q1 download

Let’s now go through GoodRx’s latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

GoodRx’s revenue grew 2.6% y/y to $203.0 million, ahead of Wall Street’s $202.2 million (+2.2% y/y) expectations. Revenue growth also accelerated meaningfully against 1.0% growth in Q4.

That being said, we acknowledge that it wasn’t a perfect quarter. One of the more worrying aspects within Q1 was the fact that the company’s count of monthly active consumers (MACS) dipped again to 6.4 million, after briefly rising to 6.6 million in Q4 and sparking hopes of stabilization. Subscription plans, however, did hold relatively steady at ~680k, primarily GoodRx Gold members as the company has now largely lapped the headwind of ending the Kroger Savings plan.

Another major possible disruption for GoodRx is the recent bankruptcy announcement of Rite Aid. Though the company is expected to sell off many of its locations as operational pharmacies, inevitably some locations will shutter, capping off GoodRx’s access to some of its consumer base. Per CEO Wendy Barnes’ remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

I want to acknowledge the ongoing uncertainty in the macroeconomic environment driven by factors that could influence our business, including regulatory and policy changes, shifting consumer sentiment, tariffs and evolving government initiatives. I also want to acknowledge the news pertaining to the Rite Aid bankruptcy announcement this week. This is recent news and we are actively working to understand more. What we know today is that they have reentered bankruptcy and are pursuing a sale of substantially all of their assets. It is too early to know how this will exactly play out, but we do believe that we are well positioned to engage consumers and other pharmacy partners to ensure smooth patient transitions. Ultimately, these prescriptions will be filled somewhere and we are in active dialogue with pharmacy partners to make sure we are best positioned to service our consumers.

I would also highlight that our efforts to broadly engage pharmacy partners over the past several months have put us in a strong position for productive dialogue with potential buyers or landing spots for these patients, and we believe that we can create unique and mutually beneficial arrangements to support such patient transitions. Despite these potential challenges, the strength of the GoodRx brand remains clear across all economic conditions.”

This only serves to highlight the importance of GoodRx’s shift to direct pharmaceutical marketing. In Q1, pharma manufacturer solutions revenue grew 17% y/y to $28.6 million, now representing ~15% of total revenue and helping to offset the y/y headwinds from a smaller subscription headcount.

GoodRx’s adjusted EBITDA also rose 11% y/y to $69.8 million, with margins rising 270bps y/y to 34.4%.

The company continues to be particularly fierce on opex management, in particular cutting sales and marketing expenses down -4% y/y (and shrinking it by 2 points as a percentage of revenue).

Risks and key takeaways

There are, of course, a minefield of risks that GoodRx investors need to consumer. The retail pharmacy business has been weak since COVID, and the bankruptcy of Rite Aid not only serves to demonstrate those challenges but also cuts off a portion of GoodRx’s customer base. The company is growing double digits via its pharmacy relationships, but so far this is still a minor portion of revenue compared to its current transactional cuts that it earns from consumers redeeming coupons in-store.

That being said, I think these risks are already more than priced into GoodRx’s stock at ~6x adjusted EBITDA. Buy here and hold out for a rebound.

data.title: Shoe Carnival: Buy This Small Cap For Exceptional Dividend Growth
data.description:
Investment Thesis

After losing 50% of its value over the last year, Shoe Carnival (NASDAQ:SCVL) is now a great buying opportunity for dividend investors looking to diversify their portfolio with an under-the-radar small cap. The company has managed to grow its dividend distribution at a compound annual growth rate of 28% over the last five years, giving investors a 5-year yield on cost of 9.95%.

With a strong balance sheet and low payout ratio, investors can feel secure knowing Shoe Carnival's 12 years of consecutive dividend payments will continue to grow.

Company Overview

Market Cap

$532.1M

5-Year Revenue Growth

4.88%

Enterprise Value

$804.5M

Return on Equity

10.52%

12-month Return (%)

-50.78%

Price/Book

0.81x

Inside Ownership

31.60%

Price/Cash Flow

7.01x

Over the last year, Shoe Carnival's stock has been hit hard - the stock price is down over 50% while the earnings are only slightly down from the peak of 2021. Aside from the current cyclical downtrend in the family retail space, there are several different investment themes taking place that have caused investors to reject the stock.

First, and most significant, the tariff announcement has had a big impact on the retail sector. Morgan Stanley estimates that retail will be the worst performing sector if the trade dispute is not resolved. On average, the investment bank expects that retailers could see as much as a 35% hit to earnings per share.

Looking beyond tariffs, investors have shunned small-cap opportunities. With the recent performance of the Mag-7, investors are migrating out of small-cap stocks to larger, more 'glamorous' large and mega caps.

The chart above from the CFA Society Blog highlights the underperformance in small caps relative to large caps - a 12-year trend that reverts during periods of high inflation or uncertainty.

The last investment theme working against Shoe Carnival is the underperformance of value vs. growth stocks. SCVL is a value stock, which as an investment theme has become out of favor in long and extended bull markets. The CFA Society publication highlights that value stocks have historically outperformed - like small caps - during periods of high interest rates and market turmoil.

As a small-cap value stock, Shoe Carnival is currently out of favor with the investing community, driven by the themes from the extended bull market mentioned above. Investors willing to look past the negative view on the company have an opportunity to buy a discounted dividend grower with upside appreciation built in.

Operations

Shoe Carnival is a discounted family footwear retailer operating primarily in the Midwest. The company sells lower-cost athletic and casual footwear across 429 stores. Of the 429 total stores, SCVL operates 334 Shoe Carnival stores, 67 Shoe Station stores, and 28 Rogan's Shoes stores.

Per the abbreviated income statement above, Shoe Carnival has grown revenue at a compound annual growth rate of 3.9% from 2016 to 2022.

The company had several years of flat to declining revenue until its late-2021 acquisition of Shoe Station. The acquisition proved to be a windfall for the company - operating margins in 2021 and 2022 were 16.0% and 11.6%, respectively. Through internal sales data, management identified that Shoe Station, a premium brand compared to Shoe Carnival, offers greater sales and profitability opportunities. Specifically, in SCVL's most recent earnings call, management said that Shoe Station is the fastest growing retailer over the last two years.

The company has since embarked on a multi-year 'rebanner' strategy, which is intended to rebrand the existing Shoe Carnival stores into Shoe Station stores. The program, first run as a pilot in 2022, is now expected to be complete by the end of 2027 as management plans to rebanner at least 80% of its Shoe Carnival stores. With several stores already under the new branding, Shoe Carnival recorded 4.9% sales growth in its Shoe Station segment, while the broader retail footwear industry saw declining revenues. Despite strong growth in Shoe Station, Q1 2025 totals sales were down 10% for on the quarter, but management expects total sales to be down only -2% for the year.

Peer Performance

In the table below I have included several of Shoe Carnival's peers, including American Eagle Outfitters (AEO), Guess?, Inc (GES), Genesco (GCO), and Designer Brands (DBI) for a comparison of growth, profitability, and valuation.

The average and median growth rates across the sector have been very meager. The median 3-year revenue growth rate is -1.4% while SCVL is 190bps lower at -3.3%.

However, you'll notice in the abbreviated income statement (shown previously) that Shoe Carnival had incredible operating performance in 2021 and 2022. Per management, Shoe Carnival's 2021 acquisition of Shoe Station led to its best operating year in history.

As the company now enters its strategic rebanner program, revenues will be down relative to the 2021/2022 peak but are expected to rebound by 2027 after the program is completed. Despite the revenue underperformance, SCVL has an operating margin and net margin at 160bps and 370bps above peers, respectively.

The margin outperformance is driven by management's disciplined approach to managing expenses (like the ones highlighted in the table above) and maintaining a strong balance sheet.

Unlike so many post-acquisition transactions, management at Shoe Carnival has limited unnecessary costs and inefficiencies. Since the acquisition of Shoe Station, Shoe Carnival has been able to limit SG&A costs to a compound annual growth rate of only 3.39%. Additionally, it has maintained a 64% average cost of goods sold.

Highlighting the importance of a strong balance sheet, Shoe Carnival's net interest expense is only 0.03% of sales. In contrast with Guess? and Designer Brands, SCVL is able to generate a mid-single digit net income margin and convert almost all of its operating income to free cash flow.

Dividends

The dividend history graph above highlights the 10-year dividend performance and capital allocation strategy SCVL has committed to. Three main themes occur from the graph which include the yield, performance, and safety of SCVL's dividends.

First, because the stock has declined over 50% in the last year, the yield has jumped to a current rate of 3.08%. The dividend yield last peaked at 2.27% in April 2020 when retailers were getting crushed by the COVID-19 lockdown. Second, SCVL has consistently paid dividends for the last 12 years and has increased the yearly dividend at a compound annual growth rate of 17.7%. Although this number is already impressive enough, the company has increased the dividend at a compound annual growth rate of 28% over the last five years - if investors purchased SCVL five years ago, their yield would now be 9.95% on the original cost basis. Third, management's conservative approach to the dividend payout ratio ensures the dividend will be sustainable going forward.

The analysis below considers SCVL's dividend safety by looking at cash on hand and cash from operations, with a scenario for the Shoe Station rebanner and one for core operations.

The first component of dividend safety is the company's existing cash. As touched on previously, management has a very conservative balance sheet. The company has cash on hand of $68 million, which could pay the expected 2025 dividend four times over. Additionally, the company has no debt (if you exclude the operating leases), marking its 20th consecutive year of funding operations exclusively from cash flows.

The second component of dividend safety is Shoe Carnival's ability to generate cash flows. In the growth capex scenario, the trailing twelve-month cash from operations is $75.9 million, while the trailing twelve-month capital expenditures are $36.3 million.

This scenario accounts for the capital expenditures needed to continue the rebanner strategy. Capital expenditures are approximately $20 million higher, which brings free cash flow to $39.3 million. Under this scenario, the dividend is still covered 2.4x before considering the $68 million of cash on hand. The scenario which excludes growth capex is intended to highlight what SCVL's dividend coverage would look like under a 'normalized' scenario. Needless to say, with $20 million less spent on capital expenditures, the free cash flow covers the dividend 2.8x before considering cash on hand.

Valuation

Although the investment thesis is primarily focused on Shoe Carnival's ability to provide investors with reliable and growing dividends, the Shoe Station rebanner strategy presents a unique opportunity for upside growth.

In the table below, I have included several assumptions based on management's guidance used for the discounted cash flow model.

In an effort to be conservative and practical, I have assumed that net sales will decline by 3% for 2025 (where management expects only a 1-2% drop). Additionally, because of the ongoing rebanner strategy, I have increased the cost of sales by 130bps and increased SG&A costs 50bps.

In the income statement projections, my conservative estimate of revenue assumes a compound annual growth rate of only 0.72% from 2024 to 2028. Additionally, my 2025/2026 margins are slightly below the last few years of operating history, but rebound in 2027/2028 (and maintained at the terminal value in the next screenshot).

Based on these assumptions, I estimate a fair value of $21.80 for Shoe Carnival - a discount of 12.9% from its current price.

The investment strategy in this scenario is to buy Shoe Carnival under the assumption the dividends will continue to grow at a reasonable pace while maintaining adequate funding to support its distribution. At Shoe Carnival's current price, investors have the opportunity to purchase a company with a well-covered, growing dividend that offers $2 per share upside appreciation.

Risks

Rebanner Strategy

The primary risk that could impede Shoe Carnival's future cash flows is its rebanner strategy. As previously mentioned, the Shoe Station segment grew its revenue by 4.9% in the quarter and is considered the fastest growing retailer over the last two years. However, the rebanner strategy has not been implemented for a long enough time period to assure investors of its durability.

If the investment were to prove unsuccessful, it is likely the company's margins would be look more like the pre-Shoe Station acquisition. As highlighted previously, operating margins and net margins from the period 2016-2020 were significantly lower, which could impact Shoe Carnival's ability to generate substantial enough cash flow to cover its dividend. Although I have confidence in the rebanner strategy's success, it is something I will be paying close attention to over the next few quarters.

Tariffs

Shoe Carnival is susceptible to rising input costs if tariffs remain in place or increase. As highlighted in the previous research piece by Morgan Stanley, retailers could see as much as a 35% hit to earnings per share.

Highlighted in JPMorgan's global research piece, investors and businesses are unsure of what to expect next in the tariff fight. The uncertainty makes it difficult for businesses to impact sales, pricing, and consumer preferences. From an investor's standpoint, the uncertainty makes it difficult to gauge future cash flows and revenue growth. Despite the looming threat, Shoe Carnival's strong balance sheet and high free cash flow generation position it well to handle the uncertain macroeconomic period.

Conclusion

As a small-cap value stock in the retail sector, Shoe Carnival has become an out of favor investment. The company has lost more than 50% of its value in the last year, but investors who are willing to take a contrarian approach should be rewarded well.

For the first time in its history, the company now has a dividend yield over 3% - all while growing the dividend at a compound annual growth rate of 28% over the last five years. Shoe Carnival's conservative balance sheet, low payout ratio and ability to generate substantial free cash flow provide investors with the security they need to know their dividends are well funded and will continue to grow.

data.title: GFLW: 2025 Gains, AUM Growth, Factor Mix Make This ETF A Buy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Continuing coverage of the VictoryShares Free Cash Flow Growth ETF (NASDAQ:GFLW), a passively managed fund with a strategy at the intersection of quality and growth, today I would like to upgrade this vehicle to a Buy.

To recap, in January of this year, while emphasizing its numerous advantages like the 25.3% weighted average 3-year free cash flow CAGR of its portfolio, I opted for a Hold rating, as GFLW had just a few weeks in the books back then, and placing a lot of hope in a debutant, even assuming a seemingly capable strategy, is precarious. Moreover, its performance had not been particularly strong.

However, since then, a few cogent reasons for an upgrade have emerged.

  • GFLW has seen a massive growth in assets under management that have risen 3.5x since January and recently surpassed a $500 million milestone, indicating robust investor interest.
  • This was in part facilitated by its decent performance this year, as it has not only beaten the S&P 500 index since my January article amid the tariff drama and other headwinds but also has delivered one of the best results in the Large Growth sub-class of the U.S. Equity class.
  • Its factor mix remains close to excellent when it comes to quality (including free cash flow) and growth exposures, plus it has a few advantages over the iShares Russell 1000 Growth ETF (IWF), which, I believe, could secure more upside in the current market environment.

So below, I would like to address these reasons in due detail as well as offer a few remarks on risks.

GFLW Strategy

But before we proceed to the discussion of the reasons for optimism, let us recap GFLW's strategy.

As explained in the prospectus available on its website, GFLW tracks the Victory Free Cash Flow Growth Index, which

... is designed to deliver exposure to equity securities of large-capitalization U.S. issuers of growth companies. To be included in the Index, these U.S. large-capitalization growth companies generally will have a positive “free cash flow growth trend” and have a high “free cash flow return on invested capital.”

Constituents are selected from the VettaFi US Equity Large/Mid-Cap 1000 Index. Neither "financial companies" nor REITs can join the index, which is understandable as the concept of FCF has limitations when applied to these industries.

The weighting method is quite complex:

The rules-based methodology assigns weightings by measuring a combination of total free cash flow and “absolute momentum,” which is defined as the absolute value of a company’s trailing 12-month, risk-adjusted total return and accounts for the magnitude of price fluctuations over the specified period without regard to whether the return is positive or negative.

There are a few constraints, which, I believe, are necessary to disperse risks more evenly. More specifically:

Individual companies are capped at 4% of the Index. The rules-based methodology also limits the amount of companies in any one sector. That is, companies in any one sector will not exceed 45% of the Index. In addition, the allocation of companies in any single sector (or “weighting”) will not exceed a weighting of more than 20% greater than the weighting of that sector in the Parent Index.

GFLW Performance

Despite the exceedingly challenging environment, GFLW has been delivering excellent results.

Since my January article, GFLW has solidly outperformed the S&P 500 index.

But why has the ETF been performing so strongly? There have been a few contributors. Comparing the holdings datasets as of January 28 and June 4, I have found out that they have an 87.8% overlap, as 82 stocks have retained their place in the portfolio. In this group, 45 stocks have delivered positive price returns, while 14 have seen gains of more than 20%. And the five main contributors were as follows:

Stock Weight (as of June 4) Price return
Palantir (PLTR) 4.44% 50.03%
Duolingo (DUOL) 0.73% 48.34%
ZScaler (ZS) 0.41% 46.52%
GE Vernova (GEV) 3.65% 35.58%
Spotify Technology (SPOT) 3.93% 34.13%

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETF. Based on the share prices as of January 28 and June 5

Next, since its inception in December 2024, GFLW has outpaced IWF, which tracks the index the fund compares its returns to, as well as the Pacer US Cash Cows Growth ETF (BUL), which has certain similarities with it strategy-wise. It has also outmaneuvered the U.S. market represented by the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV).

Moreover, in the Large Growth sub-class (the U.S. Equity class, 117 ETFs in total as of June 5), GFLW is the seventh top performer YTD, with a 7.4% price return. For a fuller context, my calculations show that its result is almost 3x higher than the median return in this sub-class, 2.51%.

GFLW Has Remarkable AUM Growth

One of the issues ETF investors should always keep an eye on is whether a vehicle they are interested in has a healthy trend in asset flows. The reason for that is simple: if AUM stagnates at a low level, the risk of a fund's liquidation increases.

In the case of GFLW, I can conclude that, at least for now, investor interest has been nothing short of robust (probably because its expense ratio of 39 bps is not too high for such a sophisticated strategy), as its AUM has recently surpassed a $500 million milestone, which is an excellent result for an ETF that has just six months in the books.

GFLW Has Convincing Factor Mix

As of June 4, there were 102 holdings in the GFLW portfolio, including cash & cash equivalents and future. 100 of them are labeled as 'COMMON STOCK' or 'COMMON STOCK LIKE' in the 'Security Type' column. As I said above, 82 of them could be found in the January version. AppLovin Corporation (APP) is the key holding in this mix, with a 4.7% weight.

Regarding sectors, investors should understand that GFLW is much more selective here than IWF and IVV, as it ignores five out of 11 GICS sectors, namely energy, financials, materials, real estate, and utilities.

I would argue that the factor mix of this portfolio is supportive of a bullish view. Why? Because its growth characteristics are much stronger than those of IVV, and there is close to nothing to criticize about its quality. Also, we see a few advantages over IWF. Let us look at the data:

Size and value factors

GFLW's is anything but inexpensive, as its metrics like the adjusted weighted average earnings yield, EV/EBITDA (adjusted for the impact of outliers with four- and triple-digit figures), and Price/Sales illustrate quite clearly. Only the free cash flow yield (which is also an indirect quality indicator) speaks in its favor.

Nevertheless, what, I believe, makes it appealing here is that its exposure to the trillion-dollar companies is smaller than those of IWF and IVV (11.6% weight vs. 56% in IWF and 35.8% in IVV), and this is reflected in its weighted average market cap. And the corollary here is that it certainly should appeal to investors who are on the lookout for growth, but in a better diversified portfolio that is not overexposed to the Magnificent Seven.

Growth

GFLW's main advantages here are

  • a spectacular 3-year free cash flow compound annual growth rate of its portfolio that dwarfs those of IWF and IVV,
  • the fact that it has the smallest exposure to companies that have seen their FCF shrank over the 3-year period,
  • its allocation to companies with a B- Quant Growth grade or higher, which is the largest in this ETF trio,
  • its weighted average forward revenue, EPS, and EBITDA growth rates that exceed those of IVV.

Also, its WA forward EPS growth rate is higher than IWF's.

As to the fact that IWF has stronger forward revenue and EBITDA growth rates, I attribute it to the impact of NVIDIA (NVDA), which has a 10.9% weight in IWF but only 2% in GFLW. I have already explained this phenomenon in the previous article, so today I would like to once again make an adjustment to prove that. More specifically, if we reduce NVDA's forward revenue growth rate to 20% from 60.3%, IWF's WA rate would drop to 12.8%, while GFLW's would be surprisingly higher at 13.8%.

Quality

For quality-focused investors, GFLW offers quite a lot.

  • For example, almost all its holdings have a B- Quant Profitability rating or higher. Both IWF and IVV have smaller allocations, though marginally.
  • Both GFLW's Return on Assets and adjusted Return on Equity are much higher than those of IVV. At the same time, IWF is ahead, but mostly because of NVDA.
  • And what impressed me most is its exposure to stocks that might be called analysts' darlings, as they have a B- EPS Revisions grade or stronger. Both IVV and IWF are well behind here.

Risks

This opportunity comes with risks, chief among them being GFLW's large growth factor exposure. On the one hand, this is an advantage the ETF can be justly proud of. On the other hand, a new round of the tariff drama, if it emerges, and its impact on the perception of inflation dynamics, monetary policy, and possibility of a recession can easily result in a new wave of growth premia adjustments across the market (in other words, P/Es would start to shrink), so it is highly likely that high achievers that dominate GFLW's portfolio would see their prices sinking. This process could be exacerbated by the fact that the ETF has almost no defensive exposure, as it has no footprint in utilities and just 66 bps allocated to consumer staples. Besides, it is not to be forgotten that GFLW is a high-beta vehicle (i.e., the weighted average 24-month beta of its portfolio is at 1.38, as per my calculations).

Final Thoughts

Considering GFLW's convincing performance this year, its impressive AUM growth, and the nicely calibrated factor mix of its portfolio, I believe it is no longer necessary to defend the neutral view, so I upgrade this ETF to a Buy.

data.title: Worried About Tariff Passthrough Onto Prices? So Are Business Execs
data.description:
By Kevin Foster, Aaron Jalca and Brent Meyer

Over the past couple of months, newswires have focused on the potential for elevated tariff rates to feed through into higher inflation and potentially affect output growth as well. Indeed, Chair Powell, in his last post-FOMC meeting press conference said, "What looks likely, given the scope and scale of the tariffs, is that… the risks to higher inflation, higher unemployment have increased."

Recent research from economists at the Atlanta Fed suggests that if firms are able to pass through all the costs of tariffs, retail prices would increase significantly - as much as 1.6 percent (depending on how effective tariff rates evolve from here).

And even at a 50 percent passthrough rate, the impact on prices would be large enough to be felt in the aggregate (0.8 percent increase in retail prices). How plausible is full passthrough? Going back to the last episode with rising tariffs in 2018, research showed that the cost of the tariffs was almost entirely passed through onto domestic prices.

Rising tariff rates pose threats to the real side of the economy as well. Another highly cited academic paper on tariffs points out that in a simple supply and demand model, "higher prices also reduce demand by domestic consumers."

In this environment, where policy changes lead to sharp increases in costs for many firms, we were curious about how firms would respond, especially in light of a potential reduction in demand that typically accompanies a price hike.

So, we turned to the Atlanta Fed's Business Inflation Expectations survey (BIE), a monthly survey of Sixth District firms that is well positioned to ask timely questions on economic conditions facing firms. In gathering information for the April 2025 BIE survey, we asked firms about their ability to pass through increased costs caused by a new economic policy without a resulting reduction in demand.

To that end, we posed two questions to firms about their ability to pass through a hypothetical cost increase to consumers. One of the questions was, "Suppose a new economic policy causes unit costs for your main good or service to increase by [10 or 25] percent, effective immediately. Based on current levels of demand for your main good or service, how much of that cost increase would your firm be able to pass through to customers?"

The survey was in fielded from April 7 through April 18, at a time when 10 percent was the baseline tariff for almost all countries, and 25 percent was the tariff on all foreign-made autos and auto parts. Some countries or regions, including China and the European Union, had much higher tariffs at the time.

The interesting twist in this line of questioning is the inclusion of the phrase "Based on current levels of demand." The interpretation here is that firms are telling us how much of the cost increase they would be able to pass through to customers before it had a negative impact on demand for that good or service.

Source: Atlanta Fed's Business Inflation Expectations survey • Download data

Although a diversity of views is apparent, on average firms tell us they expect to be able to pass through 51.1 percent of a 10 percent cost increase, and 47.3 percent of a 25 percent cost increase, without reducing current levels of demand. A closer look at the responses reveals a wide range of expected passthrough rates.

Compared to the 2018 episode, where research suggests nearly full passthrough of costs into prices, our results suggest many firms believe their customers are price-sensitive enough this time around (perhaps owing to the recent inflationary surge that isn't too far in the rearview mirror) that they cannot pass through the entire cost increase without reducing demand. A natural next step, then, is to evaluate these responses in the context of firms' perceptions of their current demand.

Figure 2 plots the distribution of firms' passthrough percentages by their strength of sales revenue growth relative to "normal." Viewing this distribution reveals that firms with about normal or above-normal sales levels expect to pass through a much higher percentage of the cost increase compared to those firms with lower-than-normal sales levels.

For context, half of firms reported having below-normal sales levels, 38 percent reported about-normal sales levels, and 12 percent reported greater-than-normal sales levels.

On average, firms that reported "much less than normal" or "somewhat less than normal" sales levels expect to pass through 45.6 percent of the 10 percent cost increase, firms that report "about normal" sales levels expect to pass through a little more than half of the cost increase, and firms reporting "somewhat" or "much" greater than normal sales expect to pass through nearly two-thirds of a 10 percent cost increase. Results were similar for the 25 percent policy-related cost increase question.

Source: Atlanta Fed's Business Inflation Expectations survey

To add further context to these results, we can look at the time series behavior of firms' quantitative sales gaps. Figure 3 shows that firms, on average, see sales levels flagging relative to normal, on the order of roughly 8 percentage points in the red.

Importantly, at the outset of the trade tensions and tariffs episode of 2018–19, firms perceived their sales levels to be about "normal" (in other words, their quantitative average sales gap was near 0 percent).

Given the relationship firms' passthrough rates have to their current levels of demand, we can infer that because perceived demand is weaker now than in the prior period of trade and tariff tensions, firms will be more hesitant to fully pass through tariff-related cost increases.

Source: Atlanta Fed's Business Inflation Expectations survey • Download data

In sum, firms with about normal or greater-than-normal sales expect to be able to pass through more of the cost increases while maintaining the same levels of demand for their goods or services, as figure 2 shows. And figure 3 shows us that those firms are more likely to be larger firms, due to their smaller sales gap compared to "normal."

In the aggregate, business executives see their current sales levels as about 8 percentage points below "normal," which is much weaker than firms' relative position entering 2018. In this environment, firms on average anticipate passing through a little more than half of a 10 percent cost increase without damaging demand.

It's not yet clear where the average tariff rate will ultimately settle, or how firms' passthrough rates will evolve from here. However, it does appear that most firms anticipate sacrificing demand should they choose to fully pass a tariff-related cost increase on to customers.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Rates Spark: Global Markets Brace For U.S. Payrolls
data.description:
By Michiel Tukker

Markets are bracing for a weak US payrolls number

Friday is all about the US payroll numbers, which consensus expects to fall from 177k to 125k, thereby already positioning for a cooling jobs market. The whisper number on Bloomberg turned more pessimistic after this week’s data, with only 111k expected. US rate dynamics are more complex than in the eurozone, with different forces acting in opposing directions. Apart from growth worries, there are also inflation and fiscal pressures to consider.

A weak US payroll number should place more emphasis on the recession risk narrative and push yields down across the curve. But both the front end of the curve and the back end can only go so far. Inflation risks limit the Fed's ability to ease in the near term, and the ongoing discussions on Trump’s tax bill keep the back end higher. Whilst the spillovers to euro rates from the US seemed limited this week, we do think a bad US payroll number can hit global market sentiment and weigh down the back end of the euro curve.

The belly of the curve can outperform amid uncertainty

The European Central Bank cut by 25bp on Thursday, as widely expected, but left markets guessing about the timing of the next move. The press conference had a more hawkish tone than markets were positioned for, and now the next cut is only fully priced in by the fourth quarter. The bigger picture hasn't changed, however, and in our base case, we expect more range-bound trading centred around a landing zone of 1.75% for the ECB.

The belly of the curve could play an important role ahead, as also signified by the 5Y point underperforming other tenors yesterday. The fact is that inflation still remains above target, which means the ECB’s hands are not entirely free to cut rates if things turn for the worse. Meanwhile, the price impact due to Trump’s trade policies is hard to predict. As such, we could see the belly of the curve outperform if eurozone growth concerns flare up again. The long end of the curve is more anchored due to German spending expectations and has less room to come down.

Friday's events and market views

US payroll numbers later in the day will be the highlight, whereby the unemployment rate is expected to remain stable at 4.2%. From the eurozone, we first get retail sales, but given the numbers are from April, we don’t expect these to be a market mover.

More interesting may be the various ECB speakers slated. Holzmann from Austria will undoubtedly voice more hawkish comments, whilst Portugal's Centeno may voice more dovish sounds. Going forward, we could see more disagreement within the Governing Council about the ECB’s next steps.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Vertical SaaS At Scale: Intapp's Next Chapter
data.description:
Intapp (NASDAQ:INTA) operates in the vertical SaaS space, which naturally comes with a more limited total addressable market compared to broader horizontal SaaS platforms. Its customer base is niche but may be more sensitive to macroeconomic slowdowns, especially in discretionary tech spending. Additionally, as the company matures, its growth rates have begun to normalize, raising questions about how much further expansion is possible without significant market share gains or product innovation. Let us dive in to see where Intapp is headed.

Continued Topline Growth in SaaS and Cloud

Intapp provides SaaS solutions to workflows in legal and financial businesses with higher security requirements. There are several module products across business development, compliance and internal operations offered through its OnePlace platform. DealCloud, one of its flagship modules, is a combination of CRM and deal management solutions, tailored for specific needs typical in financial firms. The solutions are very focused and niche for capital markets, but also applicable for legal, accounting and consulting domains.

Intapp continues its transition into a maturing vertical SaaS business, with Q3 FY2025 results highlighting continued momentum in its cloud offerings despite a deceleration in overall revenue growth. Total operating revenue grew 16.6% YoY, with roughly 65% coming from SaaS operations. SaaS revenue itself grew 28% YoY. The remaining revenue comes from maintenance and support, on-premise solutions and services (the part that is less cloud delivered as well and expected to be lower margin than SaaS).

Cloud Annual Recurring Revenue (ARR) was also up 28% YoY. The cloud delivery encompasses most of the SaaS offerings along with AI enhanced tools (like Intapp Assist) or data services (fair amount of overlap with the SaaS offerings). The continued topline growth in SaaS and cloud segments reflects a move away from license and professional services revenue towards more predictable and higher margin recurring revenue. The trailing twelve-month cloud Net Revenue Retention (NRR) was also reported as a healthy 119%.

Overall, while the deceleration from ~30% growth in 2023 to ~20% in 2024 and 16.6% now indicates plateauing revenues as Intapp matures, the earnings quality is improving with encouraging topline growth in SaaS and cloud as seen earlier. There are quite a few positives in the retention metrics and growth quality to support a buy thesis, despite the plateauing revenue.

FY2025 guidance projects $331.3 million in SaaS revenue at the mid-point, showing ongoing strength and continued momentum in its cloud transition. The company also expects a non-GAAP EPS of $0.89 (midpoint) for the year. Full-year guidance for FY2026 will be interested in the face of discretionary IT spending cuts.

Free Cash Flow Positive Even at Breakeven

Intapp’s growing gross margins approaching 75% show the scalability typical of good SaaS businesses, especially as the revenue mix shifts toward higher margin cloud subscriptions. However, the fact that EBITDA remains around breakeven suggests that the company is still reinvesting heavily in sales, R&D and customer acquisition to drive growth, rather than prioritizing short-term profitability. This is acceptable for a vertical SaaS company during scale up phases, but I am a bit worried about plateauing revenue showing some signals of potential saturation (although with earning quality improvements due to cloud earnings growth). If the gross margins and operational efficiencies don't improve further, EBITDA improvements will be pressurized. I will also keep topline growth as a factor to watch.

Intapp reported a free cash flow of ~$33m in Q3 FY2025 (FCF to revenue ratio of ~25%). This despite EBITDA being just marginally around breakeven. This could be attributed to cost and CapEx discipline, which is both good and bad news. The good being obvious discipline and the bad is that despite the discipline, EBITDA is not materially positive, raising concerns around future profitability.

Reasonable Valuation for Intapp's Positioning

For valuation analysis, I chose Veeva Systems and nCino as benchmarks for Intapp. While being in the vertical SaaS space, they are in different stages of growth and profitability. Veeva, trading at a premium forward multiple of 12.7x EV/Revenue, is a mature and highly profitable player in life sciences vertical SaaS. On the other hand, nCino (focused on financial SaaS), with a lower forward multiple of 5.3x EV/Revenue, is in its early stages of growth and profitability. Intapp, trading at a forward ratio of 8.3x EV/Revenue, is between Veeva and nCino in terms of profitability and size. The stage versus valuation numbers mostly look in line considering the three peers' range.

Despite recent valuation contractions, the multiple for Intapp is still around the higher range of its history. However, that is not concerning as gross margins are improving, and the bottom line is showing signs of breaking even with good quality earnings improvement. In fact, the comparison with Veeva shows that if Intapp can scale and grow further, there is valuation multiple expansion potential still left. Despite limitations of the total addressable market for vertical SaaS versus horizontal SaaS.

Next Steps for Intapp

The thesis so far points to the margins, top line and relative valuations reflecting fairly on share prices as of today, with its cautions and positives. Where Intapp goes from here is therefore dependent on the fundamental strength of its offerings and immediate macro factors.

Workflows in legal and finance use cases are particularly sensitive to compliance requirements and have higher security needs. It is a niche requirement and Intapp's single focus helps cater to solutions demanded by the specific segment. Generic workflow tools will need more customization to get into the space. Intapp's specialization protects some sort of moat for its offerings. However, it is not a wide moat - there is competition from several disruptive smaller start-ups. Even bigger players have the capability and resources to get in over time. Existing competition addresses parts of the whole solution provided by Intapp, in a fragmented and generic way. Salesforce for CRM, Thomson Reuter's Elite legal back office suite and iManage's document and risk management solutions are examples of generic part solutions to the overall problem.

The SaaS and cloud emphasis is also supportive for long-term growth at healthy margins. Embedding AI into workflows can drive deeper customer engagement and enhance pricing power. Intapp identifies a Serviceable Addressable Market (SAM) of $15b today (and growing, from $9.6b in 2021) in its investor presentation, May 2025. Although I have highlighted the limited target market for vertical SaaS, today Intapp has penetrated only ~3% of the SAM. That shows lots of potential to grow is execution backs the promise.

Overall, Intapp has no clear long-term moat, but its vertical specialization and switching cost barriers mean better customer retention, if not acquisition. The recent emphasis on tighter regulatory scrutiny and compliance demands for enterprises (particularly legal and financials) is a secular tailwind. That should provide some cushion against an upcoming spending cut regime. Whether Intapp's secular tailwind is deemed mission-critical or not will dictate how it navigates upcoming near term challenges. While 2025 guidance (with just a quarter out ending June 2025) is not reflecting any material change in outlook, 2026 guidance will be critical. If not a pressure downwards, expect some slowdown or stagnation in topline growth as new acquisitions suffer and existing customers resist upselling or even cut down costs.

Buy With Fuel in Tank

While Intapp's long-term growth ceiling is still quite far, as seen in the valuation analysis and strong business potential, the immediate macro environment introduces uncertainty around IT budgets and discretionary spend. This could create more attractive entry points in the near term, though valuations are not clearly stretched or compressed enough to suggest how much future risk or upside is already priced in. Prices today appear fairly valued to Intapp's fundamentals today. New bets will be on growth and profitability from here on.

Decelerating revenue growth is a concern. But high gross margins, operational discipline and AI-enhanced SaaS offerings are structural drivers for future expansion. Investors can consider some exposure to Intapp as timing the perfect entry point is difficult. Expect gains as Intapp enters consistent profitability, and be ready to accumulate on further dips. In terms of risks, keep an eye on consistent EBITDA struggles. The upcoming macro headwind could pressurize topline growth, but accumulate on further dips, unless Intapp faces a secular fall in fundamentals.

data.title: June ECB Meeting: In A Good Place To Assess The Risks
data.description:
By Seema Shah, Chief Global Strategist; Christian Floro, CFA, CMT, Market Strategist; and Magdalena Ocampo, Market Strategist

As expected, the European Central Bank (ECB) cut its policy rates yesterday for the eighth time in this cycle, marking its seventh straight cut. The interest rates on the main refinancing operations, the marginal lending facility, and the deposit facility were each lowered by 25 basis points to 2.15%, 2.40% and 2.00%, respectively.

With the cumulative easing since June last year now totaling 200bps, and with inflation showing increased signs of stabilizing towards its target, ECB President Christine Lagarde noted that the central bank is in a good position to face uncertainties ahead, notably on evolving U.S. trade policy.

While Lagarde kept the door open for additional rate cuts, the ECB has likely earned some leeway on responding to materializing trade policy risks, especially with increasing fiscal spending providing a significant offset.

Recent developments

While risks to growth remain tilted to the downside amid the ongoing trade war, several factors are keeping Eurozone growth resilient, including a solid labor market, robust balance sheets, and easing financial conditions.

A pull-forward of activity ahead of the U.S. tariffs has also buoyed growth in Q1 and is likely to provide some momentum through the rest of the year. Moreover, there have also been increased signs that inflation is likely to stabilize and move closer towards the ECB’s 2% target as wage pressures continue to ease.

As a result, ECB President Lagarde has characterized that the ECB is well-positioned to navigate the uncertainties ahead. While she left the door open for additional rate cuts, she acknowledged that the ECB is getting close to the end of the monetary policy easing cycle.

Additional rate cuts will likely depend on the evolution of the economy amid trade policy risks materializing in the months ahead, particularly with ongoing trade negotiations between the EU and the U.S. Indeed, trade policy is likely the largest source of uncertainty to policymakers currently, particularly given the erratic nature of U.S. trade policy.

Forecast changes

The updated ECB staff projections showed a notable downgrade to inflation in 2026, driven by a combination of lower energy prices and a stronger euro. Inflation is now projected to grow 1.6% in 2026, down from the previous 1.9% forecast, and 2027 inflation is forecasted to grow 2%. These projections suggest that the ECB has greater visibility and conviction on inflation reaching its 2% target on a sustained basis.

ECB growth projections were modestly downgraded for 2026, with GDP expected to grow 1.1%, slightly down from the previous 1.2%, as trade policy uncertainty is likely to further weigh on economic activity. However, the fiscal spending boost - particularly on defense and infrastructure - should provide significant stimulus to offset the negative impact of tariffs and support growth going forward.

Policy outlook

With economic activity holding up better than expected, and with inflation showing increasing signs of stabilizing closer towards the ECB’s target, the central bank is in a good position to assess incoming risks before embarking on additional rate cuts.

Investors should not get too complacent with encouraging signs from the ECB, however, as trade policy uncertainty remains extremely elevated and is likely to continue to drive the economic outlook and sentiment going forward. Therefore, while the door remains open for additional easing from the ECB this year, they may no longer come at consecutive meetings.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: FTXR: Tesla Now A Fixture For This Factor-Based Transportation ETF
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The First Trust Nasdaq Transportation ETF (NASDAQ:FTXR) has been a poor performer since its launch in September 2016, but an adjustment to its selection and weighting process in March 2023 and the emergence of Tesla (TSLA) as a key player in the automotive space makes FTXR a much different product moving forward. Though a change was desperately needed, this article explains why I'm not confident these were the right changes, and after comparing it with four other close peers, there doesn't appear to be a great opportunity today. Therefore, I've assigned it a "hold" rating, and I look forward to explaining why in further detail below.

FTXR Overview

Strategy Discussion

FTXR tracks the Nasdaq US Smart Transportation Index. First Trust provides a description of the selection process on its website, but unfortunately, the description is not accurate. I've contacted First Trust numerous times about this discrepancy for other ETFs tracking Nasdaq US Smart Indexes but have not heard back. As a workaround, I went straight to the source and provided the following summary based on the Index methodology document.

1. The selection universe is all stocks in the following subsectors, per the Industry Classification Benchmark system:

  • Auto Services
  • Tires
  • Automobiles
  • Auto Parts
  • Airlines
  • Trucking
  • Commercial Vehicles and Parts
  • Railroads
  • Railroad Equipment
  • Delivery Services
  • Commercial Vehicle-Equipment Leasing
  • Transportation Services

The first five subsectors are in the Consumer Discretionary industry, and the last seven are in the Industrials industry.

2. The Index universe is narrowed to those with at least $2.5 billion in total market capitalization and $5 million three-month average daily traded value. At least 30 securities must pass this stage before the screen is relaxed, first by market cap and then by liquidity.

3. The Index ranks remaining securities based on an aggregate composite of four statistics: gross income, return on assets, a price momentum composite (3, 6, 9, and 12 months), and cash flow less stock option expenses.

4. The Index selects securities ranking within the top 75% of the Index universe, subject to a 30-stock minimum and 50-stock maximum.

5. The Index weights based on a security's cash flow less stock option expense, subject to conditions that generally limit allocations to between 0.5% and 8.0% at each reconstitution date in March and September.

Prior to March 20, 2023, the Index selected securities based on volatility, value, and growth, based on this archived version of the same document. In addition, weights were based on a composite of these three factors instead of the current cash flow-driven approach. It's crucial that readers realize the enormous implications of these changes, especially now that Tesla (TSLA) has generated nearly $17 billion in operating cash flow over the last twelve months. That figure was -$124 million when FTXR launched, and since that's the key determinant of a security's weight, today's components look nothing like they did in 2016.

Performance Summary

Change is needed, too, because FTXR has been a poor performer since its launch, delivering only a 77.83% total return compared to 106.58% and 82.09% for the iShares Transportation Average ETF (IYT) and the SPDR S&P Transportation ETF (XTN), respectively.

Transportation stocks have performed badly against the broader Industrials sector, evidenced by the substantially higher returns for the Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Industrials ETF (RSPN) and the Industrial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLI). However, in a piece of good news for FTXR, it has outperformed its most direct peers (IYT and XTN) since its Index updated its rules. A lot has to do with Tesla (TSLA), which gained a remarkable 62.52% in 2024.

FTXR Analysis

Composition Analysis

As I mentioned earlier, FTXR's auto stocks are classified in the Consumer Discretionary sector, which is not permitted in IYT, XTN, RSPN, and XLI. The breakdown between Consumer Discretionary and Industrials is 33/67%, and it just so happens that FTXR's top three stocks are in the automobile industry, which comprises 26.81% of the portfolio.

Fundamentals By Top 25 Holdings

The next table highlights selected fundamental metrics for FTXR's top 25 holdings, which total 91.74%. Have a quick scan of these statistics, and I'll offer my takeaways afterward.

1. FTXG's weighted average operating cash flow over the last twelve months is $8.6 billion, the highest of the five ETFs. The table nicely illustrates that this figure determines each stock's weighting in the Index, but more importantly, notice how Tesla, Ford Motor (F), and General Motors (GM) generate the most cash flow by far. To be specific, they combine for 39% of the total operating cash flow of all members, so all they have to do is merely qualify for the Index, and they'll receive the maximum 8% weighting at each reconstitution. They'll easily do that, too, given how cash flow is also one of the four factors used to determine which stocks qualify.

2. FTXG's momentum screens are at least partially on display. For example, its constituents are up 12.24% over the last year on a weighted average basis, and numerous stocks, including Tesla (TSLA), United Airlines (UAL), and Ryder System (R), contribute to this strong statistic. IYT's and XTN's one-year price returns are only 6.46% and 1.89%, respectively, but these pale in comparison to RSPN and XLI's figures. Furthermore, I'm not sold on the effectiveness of the factor as applied to such a small group of stocks, and I think it would be more useful to assess momentum compared to a broad-based sector or market benchmark.

3. FTXG trades at 12.30x forward earnings and 4.85x trailing cash flow, indicating it's the best value-oriented fund of the five. However, its one-year estimated sales and growth rates are only about 3%, so it's also the worst from a growth perspective. I also confirmed this by calculating sector-adjusted value and growth scores for each ETF, which I derived from their components' Seeking Alpha Factor Grades, as follows (value, growth score):

  • FTXR: 5.71/10, 3.50/10
  • IYT: 5.18/10, 5.05/10
  • XTN: 6.37/10, 5.33/10
  • RSPN: 2.72/10, 4.53/10
  • XLI: 2.50/10, 4.82/10

When combining the two scores, IYT and XTN look superior, but I caution that the entire transportation sector is priced low for a good reason. For example, sales surprises were virtually flat for the components in FTXR, IYT, and XTN last quarter (0.15% / 0.06% / -0.74%), and I suggest that as the source of their poor momentum compared to the broader Industrials sector, where sales surprises averaged 0.66%. Weighing on FTXR's poor result is Tesla, whose 9.34% miss was the biggest in the last two years.

Since Tesla is virtually guaranteed to earn the maximum 8% weight in FTXR at each semi-annual reconstitution, that makes the fund riskier than the other two transportation ETFs, which, by design, aren't permitted to hold the stock.

Investment Recommendation

FTXR tracks a specialty Index designed to select 30-50 transportation stocks based on gross income, return on assets, price momentum, and cash flow. Unique to FTXR is its inclusion of auto stocks Tesla, Ford, and General Motors, and since its components are weighted based on cash flow, their allocations will max out at 8% at each semi-annual reconstitution. Especially in the case of Tesla, this suggests FTXR will be an above-average risky product for the foreseeable future.

Unfortunately, momentum is not with transportation stocks at the moment, and peers like IYT and XTN have also lagged the broader Industrials sector for the better part of the last nine years. FTXR's growth rates are also quite low, so despite its attractive 12.30x forward P/E, I don't see a great investment opportunity today. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Amgen's Strategic Path - Navigating Patent Expiries With Promising New Therapies
data.description:
Introduction

My last look at Amgen (NASDAQ:AMGN) was in March. My focus was on its Phase 3 obesity treatment, MariTide, which cleverly combines GLP-1 agonism with GIP antagonism in a once-monthly injection. MariTide is critical to Amgen's future, as nearly 30% of its revenue is "at risk" within the next five years.

The following article reviews Amgen's most recent financial results, its "revenue at risk," and investigational drugs. I also conducted a discounted cash flow analysis that reveals Amgen is priced for no growth, implying substantial upside should the company outperform low expectations.

Recent Developments

Amgen's portfolio is diversified, with multiple blockbuster drugs.

Here are its top performers from 2024:

Drug FY2024 Sales (in $ millions) YOY Growth (%) Primary Patent Expiry
Prolia 4,374 8 US patent expired Feb. 2025; biosimilar launches expected H2 2025
Enbrel 3,316 (10) US patents extend to 2028/2029; ongoing antitrust litigation; existing price erosion
Xgeva 2,225 5 US patent expired Feb. 2025; biosimilar launches expected H2 2025
Repatha 2,222 36 No immediate patent threat
Otezla 2,126 (3) US patents extend to 2028; impairment charge taken in Q1 2025

Notably, the top three drugs, which account for nearly $10 billion of Amgen's $33.4 billion total revenue in 2024, are vulnerable to patent cliffs.

First Quarter 2025 Performance

In its first quarter 2025 earnings report, Amgen saw its revenues grow 9% year-over-year to $8.1 billion. Beyond looming patent cliffs, Amgen is also dealing with a reduction in net selling prices. This has been an ongoing issue (see trend in "Gross Margin" card in the first image above). So, 14% volume growth was partially offset by a 6% lower net selling price. Still, Amgen's GAAP operating income improved by 1.1 percentage points to 15%. Enbrel, once a top product of Amgen, is feeling the pain. Its sales decreased 10% year-over-year in the first quarter due to a 47% drop in net selling price.

Looking ahead, Amgen projects 2025 revenues to range between $34.3 billion and $35.7 billion, with an acceleration in operating margin in subsequent quarters.

Strategic Acquisitions

Much of Amgen's growth can be attributed to its $27.8 billion acquisition of Horizon in 2023. Tepezza, for Thyroid Eye Disease, amassed $1.9 billion in 2024 sales. Its first quarter performance was more muted ($381 million, down 10% year-over-year) due to inventory adjustments. While Tepezza is trending well, relying on acquisitions to drive growth has its setbacks (see "Financial Flexibility" card).

Pipeline Progress

Thanks to solid "innovation investments," Amgen does have a few tricks up its sleeve. MariTide remains in Phase 3 for obesity without diabetes and Phase 2 for obesity with diabetes. Amgen anticipates data from Part 2 of the Phase 2 chronic weight management study later this year. Some believe MariTide could generate as much as $5 billion in peak annual revenue, which would go a long way in offsetting some revenue at risk.

Another interesting developmental drug is olpasiran, a "small interfering RNA that reduces lipoprotein(a) synthesis in the liver." Phase 2 data, published in NEJM, demonstrated the drug's ability to significantly reduce lipoprotein(a) in patients with established heart disease with subcutaneous administration every 12 weeks. Lipoprotein(a) is believed to be a "causal risk factor for atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease," and there is no currently available drug that addresses this. There are multiple drugs targeting lipoprotein(a) in development. Ionis' (IONS) once-monthly pelacarsen appears furthest in development, with Phase 3 trial data expected in the first half of 2026. It is estimated that around one in five people have elevated lipoprotein(a). This could be a multi-billion-dollar market for drugmakers, much like the $9.3 billion PCSK9 inhibitor market, which also addresses lipid and cardiovascular disease. Amgen anticipates initiating a Phase 3 trial for olpasiran within a year.

Biosimilar Initiatives

Amgen is also making investments in its biosimilar business. This is a lower-margin business compared to brand drugs, but it helps diversify revenue streams. Notably, Amgen recently launched the first FDA-approved biosimilar of Stelara for the treatment of multiple inflammatory conditions, Wezlana. It generated $150 million in first-quarter sales. Amgen is also developing biosimilars for Opdivo, Keytruda, and Ocrevus (multiple sclerosis). These are all blockbuster opportunities, but, like I said, lower margins, and there will likely be several biosimilars vying for market share. So, it's not like Wezlana is bound for glory.

Valuation Analysis and Sensitivity

Given these dynamics, it's no surprise that AMGN is conservatively valued on a cash flow basis. My discounted cash flow (DCF) model suggests that AMGN is priced for no growth.

I used revenue growth and FCF margin assumptions of 2.5% and 30%, respectively. In the trailing twelve months, Amgen's FCF margin was 33.3% (CFO less CapEx). Here are analyst revenue estimates on AMGN:

So, despite billions of dollars at risk, analysts anticipate that Amgen will have stable growth. Bear in mind that my assumptions represent averages over the nine-year projection period. Amgen's beta is lower per source, but I went with a blended one to be conservative. Everything else was gathered from SEC filings/standard DCF methodology.

Even with conservative assumptions (I'm essentially assuming that margins will continue to narrow and that Amgen has reached terminal growth), Amgen is undervalued by about 27%. This is not uncommon for large pharmaceutical companies facing large patent cliffs. Amgen's future growth relies heavily on investigational treatments like MariTide, and the outcomes of such drugs are inherently uncertain.

My sensitivity analysis also acts like a "reverse DCF model," in that it reveals the implied market assumptions of growth (assuming all other inputs remain the same).

Amgen appears priced for -0.4% revenue growth, with significant upside should it achieve mid-single-digit growth.

If you are convinced that Amgen can sustain low-single-digit growth in the next decade, the risk/reward profile is highly favorable.

Conclusion

To wrap up, Amgen faces the same problems many large pharma companies do, including inflation, drug price pressures, tariffs, and patent cliffs. The headwinds are significant, and it's no wonder that AMGN and its peers are underperforming the market. However, investigational drugs like MariTide and olpasiran offer compelling potential, and Amgen is priced for no growth. Should it exceed low expectations or have breakthroughs in the clinic, I can envision upside potential, meriting continued speculation (maintain Buy).
